title: Pretrained Models, Fine-Tuning, and RAG
concept:
Modern ML leverages large **pretrained models** (also called foundation models) — models trained on massive, general datasets (billions of text tokens, millions of images) at enormous computational cost. Rather than training from scratch, practitioners reuse these models and specialise them for specific tasks using two complementary approaches.

**Fine-tuning** involves additional training of a pretrained model on a smaller, task-specific dataset. Only some layers are updated (often just the top layers or adapters). The model's weights change. Fine-tuned models are better at specialised tasks but require retraining when the underlying knowledge needs updating. Examples: fine-tuning GPT-4 on medical literature; fine-tuning ResNet on chest X-rays.

**RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)** augments a model at **inference time** by dynamically retrieving relevant documents from an external knowledge base and injecting them into the prompt context. The model's weights are NOT changed. RAG is ideal when knowledge must be up-to-date (e.g., current legal documents, product catalogues) since the knowledge base can be updated independently of the model.

Key distinction for testing:
- Fine-tuning changes the model → requires full model re-evaluation after each fine-tuning run.
- RAG changes the retrieved context at query time → requires testing the retrieval pipeline's quality and relevance, plus verifying the model's reasoning on retrieved content.
- RAG ≠ fine-tuning; they are complementary and can be combined.
keyPoints: Pretrained foundation models are large models trained on massive datasets that can be reused rather than training from scratch.
Fine-tuning updates some model weights on task-specific data; it permanently changes the model and requires re-evaluation after each fine-tuning run.
RAG retrieves external documents at inference time without modifying model weights — useful for keeping knowledge current; it requires testing the retrieval pipeline's quality.

quiz.question: A legal tech company wants their AI assistant to answer questions using the most current case law, which changes weekly. They cannot retrain the model each week. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Fine-tuning the model on new case law each week.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), which retrieves current documents at inference time without changing model weights.
Training a new foundation model each week from scratch on all available case law.
Increasing the temperature parameter to allow the model to generate more current-sounding answers.
quiz.explanation: RAG retrieves current documents from an external knowledge base at query time, without modifying model weights. This means the knowledge base (case law repository) can be updated weekly while the model remains unchanged. Fine-tuning would require weekly model updates and full re-evaluation cycles, which is cost-prohibitive.
